name: Value Selling Framework
summary: Value Selling focuses on creating, quantifying, and communicating business value throughout the entire sales cycle. It starts with a value hypothesis, builds a business case with hard numbers, and ensures every conversation is anchored to measurable outcomes rather than product features.
whenToUseIt: Use Value Selling when dealing with financially sophisticated buyers (CFOs, COOs, procurement), when selling higher-priced solutions that need ROI justification, or when competing against cheaper alternatives. It's essential for any deal where the buyer needs to build an internal business case to get budget approved. If you can't quantify your value, you'll always compete on price.
steps: Develop a Value Hypothesis — Before your first conversation, hypothesise how your solution creates value for this specific buyer. Research their industry benchmarks, public financials, and common pain points. Example: 'Based on their size and industry, they're likely losing $X to inefficiency in Y process.'

Validate Through Discovery — Test your hypothesis in discovery. Ask questions that uncover the real numbers: 'How much time does your team spend on X?' 'What's the cost when Y goes wrong?' 'How much revenue is at risk from Z?' Replace your assumptions with the buyer's actual data.

Build the Business Case — Create a simple, compelling financial model: (1) Current cost of the problem (annual). (2) Expected improvement with your solution (based on benchmarks and customer results). (3) Investment required (your price). (4) Net value / ROI / payback period. Keep it on one page.

Sell to the Economic Buyer — Your champion may love the product, but the economic buyer approves the budget. Translate everything into their language: ROI, payback period, total cost of ownership, opportunity cost. 'For every month you delay, the problem costs $X.'

Reinforce Value Post-Sale — After closing, track the value delivered. Share quarterly value reports: 'In Q1, we saved your team 320 hours and reduced churn by 8%, delivering $180K in value against your $48K investment.' This drives renewals, expansion, and referrals.
example:
You sell a revenue intelligence platform to a VP of Sales at a $40M SaaS company.

Value Hypothesis: Their team is likely losing forecast accuracy because deal inspection, call review, and manager coaching all happen inconsistently. Based on similar teams, that usually means forecast misses, slower rep ramp, and weak late-stage control.

Discovery reveals: The team carries $18M in annual new-business target, managers spend hours each week stitching together call notes, and commit has missed by more than 15% in two of the last three quarters.

Business Case:
- Problem cost: poor planning confidence plus missed revenue timing
- Platform cost: $90K/year
- Expected improvement: tighter forecast discipline, faster manager inspection, better rep ramp
- Commercial logic: one prevented quarter-end miss or one additional closed enterprise deal can cover the investment
- Payback period: credible within the first year under conservative assumptions

The buyer does not need hype. They need a clear link between the current operating pain and the financial value of fixing it.
